BEARING 315
The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest
July, 1994

Sacajawea Orienteers Progress Report

Elis Eberlein, President

Dear SACO members and others,
This is a little progress report about the activities what has been going lately. The club now has a new version of the Sacajawea State Park map in the scale 1:10000. Unfortunately few people showed up to test it on May 7. A special thanks to Mike Schuh for letting me use his computer system and apartment to finish it up. I am presently doing the field work on a new map at Horn Rapids County Park. This park is about 5 km long and about ½ km wide along the Yakima River. This area makes it possible to set better and longer courses. The map scale will be 1:15000. If I have time this summer I will also do fieldwork for maps of the Richland water front (Howard Ampn and Leslie Grove parks) and Columbia Park in Kennewick.

Rumors are also around that SACO is holding an A-meet in May 1996 in the Juniper Dunes area. Unfortunately, Juniper Dunes is a Wilderness Area and the management plan does not allow for organized competition in the area. However, BLM also owns large pieces of land just outside the wilderness which they suggested that we look into. I hope to be able to visit those in the late summer or fall.

Another progress is that the club now owns the material for organizing events (control markers, punches etc.) thanks to the generosity of the Washington State Orienteering Association. WSOA also granted us money to purchase a copy of OCAD for map making. As most members of SACO are new to orienteering I will try to make as many of you as possible to go to the "real" A-meet at Cle Elum on September 10-11. It is a great opportunity to really get into this sport and meet the great people that do this and won't be any closer for a while. I promise to call and harass you (if necessary) about it.

Summer Training

Cascade Orienteering's training coordinator, Eric Bone, has organized a series of training events to help you train for the Cle Elum "A" event, the Washington State Champs, or begin to get ready for the WIOL season this winter. All events are to be held at Hamlin Park and will be informal (no registration and timing) and free. However, if you do not have a map of the park, you may need to buy one (we'll have some with us). The July 16 event will be on a 1:10000 version of the map of the north woods only with 3-4 loop courses between 1-3 km each available. The format of the other events are uncertain at this point. The training events will be suitable for anyone who is familiar with basic orienteering techniques and wants to get out and run a bit. The training will be held on:

  • Sat., July 16, 10-12
  • Sat., August 6, 10-12
  • Wed., August 24, 6-7:30pm
  • Wed., September 14, 6-7:30pm

Not For The Faint Of Heart Nor Foot

(DCL Rogaine III Review)

31 teams accepted the challenge from Rogaine course setters Paul Magus and Bill Cusworth and were treated to an enjoyable, though demanding, event at the third annual Drunken Charlie Lake Rogaine.

Being one hour East of Seattle, you might image that the map area is not all white nor flat and you would be right! So a six, or even 3, hour event is no piece of cake. (One solid master mixed team, participating for there first year weren't sure if the would be able to navigate

20 or 30 km in the six hours at a strong hiking pace; they ended up doing about 10.)

But the word must be spreading that this event is worth the drive, or flight, for this year, for the first time, we had multiple teams from British Columbia and participants from Oregon and Alaska. (Next year we may really have to enforce the 40 pre-registered/10 day-of-event team limit.)

Well, with one-half of the Dan Waugh/Mark Hartinger 1993 US DCL champs "goofing off" electing to do a 50 mile trail run event, Dan was fixed up with another great partner with Mark's help. Bottom line, even though the Waugh/Scharffenberg team had a average age of 50 plus, they set the standard bagging 880 points (out of 1060) and by getting 17 (of 22) controls. (See Dan's map and companion article.)

On their heels (scoring wise with 800 points, but taking a different course approach) was Steve Chatwin and Kriss Darntell, competing for the first time at the DCL Rogaine. While they "felt strong out there" they were "surprised that their score was good enough for second over-all". Are they going to be tough next year or what!

The most contested class was the men's 6 hour and the Canadian Tranbarger/Faris team with 760 points bested Andy and Jens Gundah (the Scandinavian "ringer") by a mere 20 points. And on the 3 hour course, last year's champs of Navy Seal Arne Skog and Microsoft Wizard Peter Golde finished two place back from first timers Ben Forward and Mike Schuh. (The Bone boys earned more gross points, but elected to take a "side trip home and giving up 150 points.)

As always the food was great: Linda's lentil stew; Great Harvest Fresh Apple Walnut Bread, fruit and drink of all kinds, and grilled sausages hit the spot afterwards. The social aspect was cut short after the awards owning to some less than pleasant weather, but that seemed to be the only short coming. So, to those that met the challenge this year, congratulations. Here's to the 1995 DCL Rogaine, hope to see you there. (Again, a special thank to Paul and Bill for setting such a nice course.)

PICNIC! PICNIC! PICNIC!

The annual picnic for Seattle area orienteers will be held again this year at the Lake Washington home of Don Atkinson on Sunday, August 7, starting at 2 p.m. It will be preceded by a short course or map-hike through St. Edward Park.

The course will open at 2 p.m. Start will be at the Arrowhead School. The school is on Juanita Drive, several hundred meters north of the regular entrance to St. Edward. There will be both a regular course (regular fee, timed, and a color map) and a map hike (no fee, not timed unless you wish, old map and controls by trails).

For those who wish to proceed directly to the picnic, follow Arrowhead Drive (the road you turned off of to get to the school) to a dead end at the lake. Don's house will be marked by controls and the finish. Since parking is limited, we encourage those running to leave their cars by the school, or to park in any open area at the top of the hill.

Pop and hot dogs will be provided. Otherwise it is a pot-luck. Swimming and water activities, with boat trips for the strenuously disadvantaged.

This is purely a fun event, open to all orienteers and friends from any club. Hope to see you there.

Locals to Participate in International O-Events

Congratulations to the following Northwest orienteers:
Eric (Husky and COC) and Nick Bone (COC) will represent the U.S. at the Junior World Championships in Poland July 12-16. Eric will then head to Norway and Denmark in August to compete in World Cup Orienteering events #4, #5, and #6. Eric was also named to the World University Championships team but will not attend that meet.

Ellen Boomer (UPS and Nisqually) and Lisa Nordlund (Western Washington U and COC) will travel to Switzerland in September to represent the U.S. at the World University Championships.

Debbie Newell (COC) will compete for the U.S. in World Cup events #7, #8, and #9 in Germany and the Czech Republic September 25-October 1. Linda Hood (COC) will accompany the U.S. Team as Team Leader.

Because of his strong showing at the U.S. Intercollegiate Championships, University of Washington student Jon Witmer was named as an alternate to the World University Championships team.

Summer WIOL Series

This summer, we are holding several meets at which there will be WIOL courses, with help for beginners, and a chance for those running cross country this fall or WIOL this winter to get in some early practice. There are also a series of training meets at Hamlin Park this summer which should also appeal (see separate article this issue). There are three meets scheduled:

  • July 23, Saturday, NAD Park, Bremerton, starts 10-noon.
  • July 30, Saturday, Ros*Elum Ridge, Cle Elum. This is day 1 of a two day meet.
  • August 6, Sunday, St. Edward Park, Bothell (see picnic article). All WIOL members are invited to stay for the picnic as guests (that means you don't have to bring any food).

Regular WIOL fees will be in effect. There will be an award ceremony at the picnic.

Husky O-Team Honored

[The following text is excerpted from the UW Daily Sports section, June 3, page 21. Congratulations to the Husky team's strong write-in campaign. (Unfortunately, Eric's ranking above Napolean Kaufmann has not yet resulted in a multi-million dollar pro contract.)]

All the votes have been tallied. The countless hours of work and sweat. You the student body of the University of Washington have sounded off for your favorite athletes and moments. Here are your picks for the 1994 Daily Sports Awards. Write-in votes are denominated by a (W).

Moment of the Year
1st place — Football team's comeback win over Cal. (20 votes).
2nd place — Husky Orienteering Club wins second consecutive national title (W, 19 votes) On April 9th and 10th, four members of the Husky Orienteering Club (Rune Hoel, Eric Bone, John Whitmer and Bill Cusworth) defended the team's national title by defeating Army by 19 minutes (289:30 to 308:49). Orienteering is the sport of cross-country navigation using a map and compass.
3rd place — Crew sweeps races at Pacific Coast Championships (11 votes).

Team of the Year
1st place — Baseball (19 and a half votes)
2nd place — Husky Orienteering Club (W, 19 votes)
3rd place — Crew (16 votes)

Male Athlete of the Year
1st place — Eric Bone (W, 18 votes). Eric won his second consecutive individual national title at the U.S. Intercollegiate Orienteering Championships in early April.
2nd place — Napoleon Kaufman, football (13 votes)
3rd place — Brett Merrick, baseball (10 votes)

Cle Elum Afternoon Start Update

Rick Hood, Meet Director

We are pleased to announce, by popular request, we have added an additional level of excitement to the US Short Course Championships to be held at Cle Elum Ridge in Washington State this Sept. 10th.
While start times for the morning event will be pre-assigned, the afternoon start times will be based on the reverse order of your class finish for the morning.
For example: You are running Men's 35 and finish 3rd in your class, out of 8, for the morning event. In the afternoon, you will be the third to last in your class to start. (The openness of the area, coupled with the short distance/winning times, don't make a chase start a viable option at Cle Elum for the US Short Course Champs, we need the 2 minute spacing between competitors.)
Because of this enhancement to the afternoon event, we will need to move back the first afternoon start from 1pm to 2pm (as there must be a 3 hour time space from each competitor's morning and afternoon).
Anyway, it going to be a super time and hope you find this enhancement as exciting as we do.

Field Notes

Rick Hood

  • Our big Cle Elum event this Sept. 10/11 is just around the corner. We are working hard to make it a great time for everyone, especial those that come down from Canada and fly in from other parts of the US. To this end, we'd love your help. If you are willing to help out, please give Rick a call at 367-2606. Regardless, we sure hope you don't miss the big event. Peter Golde and Rick Breseman, course setter and vetter, have been doing a bang up job; so come enjoy the fun of quality competition or just recreate on this top notch course—the choice is yours. Oh, big reminder, no day-of-event registration save for entry and intermediate level courses; besides get your registration in soon and get the best price.

  • A big thanks to Robert and Sandy Kish Miller, who made a sizable contribution, through the United Way campaign, to Cascade OC. Is this great or what! And they have given more than just funds to support orienteering here in the Pacific Northwest: Robert is a past COC president and Sandy is the current COC treasure. So on behalf of COC, an ongoing thank you to the Millers.

  • Eric and Nick Bone are off competing in Europe, representing the US in the Student Orienteering Championships. Eric was also features twice in the Anchorage Newspapers while orienteering at the US Championship last Month. (But there is no truth to the rumor that we are thinking of changing the name of the club to "Eric OC.") Besides it wasn't just Eric that had a super showing at the Colorado Five-Day; congrats to Bill Cusworth, Paul Magnus, and the other Husky OC standouts. (PS: We hope to have an article from the "guys that ran strong" next issue.)

  • For those seeking a fine adventure, closer to home, don't pass up the Sammamish OC 2-day meet this July 30/31st. It's going to be a hot course, with either John Rogers or Mark Howlett playing a major role with the course setting. (It also might be a tad on the warm side, so taking some of your own water on the course wouldn't be a bad idea either.) So return to the site of the 1992 2-day A meet hosted by the same quality club, and get in some super orienteering in preparation for the Cle Elum Ridge US Short Course Champs this Sept.

  • Look for the State Champs to be part of the Inland Empire Cup at Riverside State Park in Spokane this fall. The course there are always fast and fun; Eastern Washington OC sure knows how to host a nice event.

  • We had some 15 plus teams paddle through the June Canoe O course, but results are a little slow in coming in; so may a 15 way tie for most fun isn't too bad a temporary standings report. (One team even decided to practice their Eskimo roll in a canoe; one member lost his wallet but "hung on to the important stuff," his map.) Oh, and next time we have a trim course, all the marker ribbons will be pink with a number as the confirmation check.

O' Suit Update

We are planning to order more Washington State O' suits within the next month or so. If you would like to order one, please call Debbie Newell (488-7471). The cost will be approximately $40.00 per suit with a deposit of $20.

USOF Short Course Championships and One-Day A Meet

Cle Elum Ridge, Washington State
September 10th & 11th, 1994

Entry Form: Please use the standard USOF entry form, on the reverse side or in a past issue of O/NA. If you have any questions, please call one of the organizers. Please mail entry form to, with check made payable to "COC" to Registrar: Don Atkinson 5826 NE 151st Bothell, WA 98011. (Those pre-registered by August 28 will receive a competitor's packet by mail.)

Location: Cascade OC extends a warm invitation for you to join us for a great fall weekend in Washington State at Cle Elum Ridge on, approximately 90 minutes East of Seattle. (Take I-90 East to exit #85 and follow O markers.)

Terrain & Map: The rolling hills and light forest of Cle Elum Ridge should make for fast, enjoyable, and exciting events, where route choice and control approach are the watchwords. The 1990 1:15,000 map (fieldwork by Are Kristiansen/Vidar Strand and cartography by Debbie Newell) is being given a light update (fieldwork by Mike Schuh and cartography by Debbie Newell) for this event. The 1990 map is available for $3.

Saturday's USOF Short Course Championship Event: Participants will run a short course of approximately one half to one third of a normal championship length and time in the morning (with starts from 10am to noon) and another of similar length and difficulty in the afternoon (with starts from 1pm to 3pm). Times will be added to determine championship standing. Participants will be pre-assigned start times for the morning and seeded times (based on morning performance) in the afternoon. Awards will be presented at Saturday's Banquet to the top 3 finishers in each "A" class.

Sunday's USOF A Meet: Participants may register for any of the standard courses, plus, for those seeking to push the envelope beyond blue, there will be an elite length "purple" course. (Men's elite course will be about 14.5 km and the elite women will use the blue course.) Elite conpetitors should register for classes M21E and F21E. Starts will be pre-assigned (9am to 11am) and awards given to top three competitors in each "A" class around 2pm.

Meet Fees: For entries postmarked by August 28, cost per day of event is $10 for USOF/COF members and $12 for others. There is a $10 late fee for entries posted after August 28. Results will be mailed for $1. There will be no day-of-event competitive registration. (Please note exception: White/Yellow recreational course participation does not require pre-registration and the course fee is $5 per day.)

Event Center & Lodging/Meals: We have exclusive use of Camp Koinonia, approximately 20 minutes West of the map (and closer to Seattle) from Friday through Sunday. Camping is $3.50/night/person and cabin bunks are $12/night/person on Friday and Saturday, both offer hot showers and refreshing pond swimming. Saturday and Sunday Breakfast is $4 and sack lunches are $3. Saturday's Banquet, limited to the first 125, is $7. Alternatively, two housing options in Cle Elum are the Timber Lodge (509 674-5966) and the Stewart Lodge (509 674-4548) with rooms ranging from $40 (for one) to $58 (for two queen beds for four).

Souvenirs: US 1994 Short Course Champs "T" shirts (short sleeve, of course) are available on a pre-order basis at $10 and $15 at the event center, while supplies last.

Child care: There will be "sharecare" hosted by COC while you are out on the course, for no charge. We will have a string/sticker course set up as well.

Flavor: Our overriding thought is to make this a quality yet simple event that you will be glad you attended. To that end, if we can help you with any special needs, please let us know on your entry form.

Primary Meet Staff: Meet Director, Rick Hood (206 367 2606); Course Designer, Peter Golde; Course Vetter, Rick Breseman; and Registrar Don Atkinson (206 488-0270).

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

What equipment do I need?

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Event Fees

Event fees are charged on a per-registration basis: two people running together with the same map and punch card pay a single fee. Additional maps are available; the charge varies depending on the map. Fees vary, but the following are typical fees for local ("C") events with color maps. Beyond Orienteering

Mark Hartinger (COC) completed the Western States 100 Trail Run in California on June 26. Mark overcame nausea to complete the course in 27:04 (that's 27 hours) and receive a coveted finishers belt buckle, awarded to those who finish in under 30 hours. Mark had to drop out of the race last year and was determined to finish this year. He has trained for 2 years and should be congratulated for his impressive accomplishment.

Debbie Newell won the women's division at the Park to Park 8k in Olympia on May 31 (34:45 on a hilly course), the Midnight Solstice 5k (20:59 on trails) in Anchorage, Alaska on June 21, and the Sultan Shindig 10k (42:04) on July 9.

On Wednesday, May 11, the Husky Orienteering Club won the University of Washington All-University Intramural Track Meet. Our nickname was "The Navigators." We were a force to be reckoned with as we received a ribbon in every event we entered, taking approximately half of all ribbons given out. They each received an intramural sports T-shirt for winning. What is USOF?

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!

block_index.text: BEARING 315 The Newsletter of Orienteering in the Pacific Northwest July, 1994

Dear SACO members and others, This is a little progress report about the activities what has been going lately. The club now has a new version of the Sacajawea State Park map in the scale 1:10000. Unfortunately few people showed up to test it on May 7. A special thanks to Mike Schuh for letting me use his computer system and apartment to finish it up. I am presently doing the field work on a new map at Horn Rapids County Park. This park is about 5 km long and about ½ km wide along the Yakima River. This area makes it possible to set better and longer courses. The map scale will be 1:15000. If I have time this summer I will also do fieldwork for maps of the Richland water front (Howard Ampn and Leslie Grove parks) and Columbia Park in Kennewick.

Rumors are also around that SACO is holding an A-meet in May 1996 in the Juniper Dunes area. Unfortunately, Juniper Dunes is a Wilderness Area and the management plan does not allow for organized competition in the area. However, BLM also owns large pieces of land just outside the wilderness which they suggested that we look into. I hope to be able to visit those in the late summer or fall.

Another progress is that the club now owns the material for organizing events (control markers, punches etc.) thanks to the generosity of the Washington State Orienteering Association. WSOA also granted us money to purchase a copy of OCAD for map making. As most members of SACO are new to orienteering I will try to make as many of you as possible to go to the "real" A-meet at Cle Elum on September 10-11. It is a great opportunity to really get into this sport and meet the great people that do this and won't be any closer for a while. I promise to call and harass you (if necessary) about it.

Cascade Orienteering's training coordinator, Eric Bone, has organized a series of training events to help you train for the Cle Elum "A" event, the Washington State Champs, or begin to get ready for the WIOL season this winter. All events are to be held at Hamlin Park and will be informal (no registration and timing) and free. However, if you do not have a map of the park, you may need to buy one (we'll have some with us). The July 16 event will be on a 1:10000 version of the map of the north woods only with 3-4 loop courses between 1-3 km each available. The format of the other events are uncertain at this point. The training events will be suitable for anyone who is familiar with basic orienteering techniques and wants to get out and run a bit. The training will be held on:
Not For The Faint Of Heart Nor Foot
31 teams accepted the challenge from Rogaine course setters Paul Magus and Bill Cusworth and were treated to an enjoyable, though demanding, event at the third annual Drunken Charlie Lake Rogaine.

Being one hour East of Seattle, you might image that the map area is not all white nor flat and you would be right! So a six, or even 3, hour event is no piece of cake. (One solid master mixed team, participating for there first year weren't sure if the would be able to navigate

20 or 30 km in the six hours at a strong hiking pace; they ended up doing about 10.)

But the word must be spreading that this event is worth the drive, or flight, for this year, for the first time, we had multiple teams from British Columbia and participants from Oregon and Alaska. (Next year we may really have to enforce the 40 pre-registered/10 day-of-event team limit.)

Well, with one-half of the Dan Waugh/Mark Hartinger 1993 US DCL champs "goofing off" electing to do a 50 mile trail run event, Dan was fixed up with another great partner with Mark's help. Bottom line, even though the Waugh/Scharffenberg team had a average age of 50 plus, they set the standard bagging 880 points (out of 1060) and by getting 17 (of 22) controls. (See Dan's map and companion article.)

On their heels (scoring wise with 800 points, but taking a different course approach) was Steve Chatwin and Kriss Darntell, competing for the first time at the DCL Rogaine. While they "felt strong out there" they were "surprised that their score was good enough for second over-all". Are they going to be tough next year or what!

The most contested class was the men's 6 hour and the Canadian Tranbarger/Faris team with 760 points bested Andy and Jens Gundah (the Scandinavian "ringer") by a mere 20 points. And on the 3 hour course, last year's champs of Navy Seal Arne Skog and Microsoft Wizard Peter Golde finished two place back from first timers Ben Forward and Mike Schuh. (The Bone boys earned more gross points, but elected to take a "side trip home and giving up 150 points.)

As always the food was great: Linda's lentil stew; Great Harvest Fresh Apple Walnut Bread, fruit and drink of all kinds, and grilled sausages hit the spot afterwards. The social aspect was cut short after the awards owning to some less than pleasant weather, but that seemed to be the only short coming. So, to those that met the challenge this year, congratulations. Here's to the 1995 DCL Rogaine, hope to see you there. (Again, a special thank to Paul and Bill for setting such a nice course.)

The annual picnic for Seattle area orienteers will be held again this year at the Lake Washington home of Don Atkinson on Sunday, August 7, starting at 2 p.m. It will be preceded by a short course or map-hike through St. Edward Park.

The course will open at 2 p.m. Start will be at the Arrowhead School. The school is on Juanita Drive, several hundred meters north of the regular entrance to St. Edward. There will be both a regular course (regular fee, timed, and a color map) and a map hike (no fee, not timed unless you wish, old map and controls by trails).

For those who wish to proceed directly to the picnic, follow Arrowhead Drive (the road you turned off of to get to the school) to a dead end at the lake. Don's house will be marked by controls and the finish. Since parking is limited, we encourage those running to leave their cars by the school, or to park in any open area at the top of the hill.
Pop and hot dogs will be provided. Otherwise it is a pot-luck. Swimming and water activities, with boat trips for the strenuously disadvantaged.
This is purely a fun event, open to all orienteers and friends from any club. Hope to see you there.

We are pleased to announce, by popular request, we have added an additional level of excitement to the US Short Course Championships to be held at Cle Elum Ridge in Washington State this Sept. 10th. While start times for the morning event will be pre-assigned, the afternoon start times will be based on the reverse order of your class finish for the morning. For example: You are running Men's 35 and finish 3rd in your class, out of 8, for the morning event. In the afternoon, you will be the third to last in your class to start. (The openness of the area, coupled with the short distance/winning times, don't make a chase start a viable option at Cle Elum for the US Short Course Champs, we need the 2 minute spacing between competitors.) Because of this enhancement to the afternoon event, we will need to move back the first afternoon start from 1pm to 2pm (as there must be a 3 hour time space from each competitor's morning and afternoon). Anyway, it going to be a super time and hope you find this enhancement as exciting as we do.

USOF Short Course Championships and One-Day A Meet
Cle Elum Ridge, Washington State September 10th & 11th, 1994

Entry Form: Please use the standard USOF entry form, on the reverse side or in a past issue of O/NA. If you have any questions, please call one of the organizers. Please mail entry form to, with check made payable to "COC" to Registrar: Don Atkinson 5826 NE 151st Bothell, WA 98011. (Those pre-registered by August 28 will receive a competitor's packet by mail.)

Location: Cascade OC extends a warm invitation for you to join us for a great fall weekend in Washington State at Cle Elum Ridge on, approximately 90 minutes East of Seattle. (Take I-90 East to exit #85 and follow O markers.)

Terrain & Map: The rolling hills and light forest of Cle Elum Ridge should make for fast, enjoyable, and exciting events, where route choice and control approach are the watchwords. The 1990 1:15,000 map (fieldwork by Are Kristiansen/Vidar Strand and cartography by Debbie Newell) is being given a light update (fieldwork by Mike Schuh and cartography by Debbie Newell) for this event. The 1990 map is available for $3.

Saturday's USOF Short Course Championship Event: Participants will run a short course of approximately one half to one third of a normal championship length and time in the morning (with starts from 10am to noon) and another of similar length and difficulty in the afternoon (with starts from 1pm to 3pm). Times will be added to determine championship standing. Participants will be pre-assigned start times for the morning and seeded times (based on morning performance) in the afternoon. Awards will be presented at Saturday's Banquet to the top 3 finishers in each "A" class.

Sunday's USOF A Meet: Participants may register for any of the standard courses, plus, for those seeking to push the envelope beyond blue, there will be an elite length "purple" course. (Men's elite course will be about 14.5 km and the elite women will use the blue course.) Elite conpetitors should register for classes M21E and F21E. Starts will be pre-assigned (9am to 11am) and awards given to top three competitors in each "A" class around 2pm.

Meet Fees: For entries postmarked by August 28, cost per day of event is $10 for USOF/COF members and $12 for others. There is a $10 late fee for entries posted after August 28. Results will be mailed for $1. There will be no day-of-event competitive registration. (Please note exception: White/Yellow recreational course participation does not require pre-registration and the course fee is $5 per day.)

Event Center & Lodging/Meals: We have exclusive use of Camp Koinonia, approximately 20 minutes West of the map (and closer to Seattle) from Friday through Sunday. Camping is $3.50/night/person and cabin bunks are $12/night/person on Friday and Saturday, both offer hot showers and refreshing pond swimming. Saturday and Sunday Breakfast is $4 and sack lunches are $3. Saturday's Banquet, limited to the first 125, is $7. Alternatively, two housing options in Cle Elum are the Timber Lodge (509 674-5966) and the Stewart Lodge (509 674-4548) with rooms ranging from $40 (for one) to $58 (for two queen beds for four).
Souvenirs: US 1994 Short Course Champs "T" shirts (short sleeve, of course) are available on a pre-order basis at $10 and $15 at the event center, while supplies last.
Child care: There will be "sharecare" hosted by COC while you are out on the course, for no charge. We will have a string/sticker course set up as well.

Flavor: Our overriding thought is to make this a quality yet simple event that you will be glad you attended. To that end, if we can help you with any special needs, please let us know on your entry form.
Primary Meet Staff: Meet Director, Rick Hood (206 367 2606); Course Designer, Peter Golde; Course Vetter, Rick Breseman; and Registrar Don Atkinson (206 488-0270).

Where and what are these Orienteering Clubs?

The Orienteering clubs listed here are independently administered non-profit organizations chartered by the United Stated Orienteering Federation. Sammamish primarily operates in the east-side (Bellevue and Redmond) area, while Cascade (the largest club) operates in the greater Seattle area. Ellensburg is centered in that town, Sacajawea is based in the Tri-Cities area, and the new Chuckanut O.C. is based in Bellingham. Husky Orienteering is for University of Washington students, faculty, and staff. Cascade and Sammamish clubs hold orienteering events throughout the year in the Puget Sound area, and eastward into the Cascade Mountains; the other clubs hold events in their areas. Membership is open to everyone, and entitles you to event discounts, club voting rights, and includes a subscription to Bearing 315, published six times per year.

The only requirement is a compass. The best kind is a protractor-type with a clear plastic base so that you can see through it to the map underneath. Compasses are available for rent at orienteering events if you don't own one. At each event you will receive a detailed topographic map of the area, usually produced specifically for orienteering by the club. Long pants (light nylon is best, but jeans will do) and long-sleeve shirts are recommended because of sometimes dense underbrush. Jogging shoes or lightweight hiking boots are preferable; your feet may get wet and muddy, so a spare pair socks and shoes are nice. Bring a watch so you can keep track of the time.

Often in this newsletter, or in orienteering conversation, you will hear references to "yousoff," and you may have seen the magazine "Orienteering/North America." But why do they need so much money, you ask? We charge $10/year for local dues, so why should it cost an extra $25 to join an organization that doesn't seem to do much? The answer, of course, is that USOF does quite a bit.

The largest part of USOF dues goes for the O/NA subscription. This is not only a great source of information, it is well written and edited, and has many articles of general interest on health, exercise, and training. From time to time, we reprint articles from O/NA, and there are always copies available at major meets and club meetings. Most of the rest of USOF dues goes to pay for insurance. Although we, as individuals, might be willing to waive insurance, virtually all land owners, both public and private, require insurance as a precondition even to talking about using their land for orienteering. USOF members get a small rebate, about $2 per national meet, to help balance out the insurance, but, basically, USOF subsidizes our meets. Without USOF, I doubt that either Cascade or Sammamish could afford the insurance to hold another meet.

So we encourage you not only to join your local club, but to join and support USOF. The magazine along is worth the cost. You will also find as many opportunities to serve on national committees as there are to serve locally. USOF, like our local clubs, has no paid employees, and does not pay travel or per diem expenses for its officers. USOF, like Cascade, is an IRS section 501c(3) corporation. Which means that donations, including out-of-pockets costs, are fully tax deductible. JOIN NOW!
